{{Short description="Equation of state of a hypothetical ideal gas"}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Ideal gas law
| image = Boyle's law final.png
| caption = [[Boyle's law]]: at constant temperature, <math>P \propto 1/V</math>.
| formula = <math>PV = nRT = Nk_B T</math>
| discovered_by = [[Benoît Paul Émile Clapeyron|Émile Clapeyron]] (1834)
}}

The '''ideal gas law''', also called the '''general gas equation''', is the [[equation of state]] of a hypothetical [[ideal gas]]. It is a good approximation of the behavior of many [[gas]]es under many conditions, although it has several limitations. It was first stated by [[Benoît Paul Émile Clapeyron|Émile Clapeyron]] in 1834 as a combination of the empirical [[Boyle's law]], [[Charles's law]], [[Avogadro's law]], and [[Gay-Lussac's law]].

== Statement ==
:<math>PV = nRT</math>

where:
* <math>P</math> = absolute [[pressure]] (Pa)
* <math>V</math> = [[volume]] (m³)
* <math>n</math> = amount of substance (mol)
* <math>R = 8.314\,462\,618\,\text{J/(mol·K)}</math> = [[gas constant|universal gas constant]]
* <math>T</math> = absolute [[temperature]] (K)

In terms of the number of molecules <math>N</math>:
:<math>PV = Nk_B T</math>
where <math>k_B = 1.380\,649 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}</math> is the [[Boltzmann constant]].

== Historical development ==
The ideal gas law unified four empirical gas laws:

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Law !! Discoverer !! Relationship
|-
| [[Boyle's law]] || 1662 || <math>P \propto 1/V</math> (constant <math>T, n</math>)
|-
| [[Charles's law]] || 1787 || <math>V \propto T</math> (constant <math>P, n</math>)
|-
| [[Gay-Lussac's law]] || 1809 || <math>P \propto T</math> (constant <math>V, n</math>)
|-
| [[Avogadro's law]] || 1811 || <math>V \propto n</math> (constant <math>P, T</math>)
|}

== Microscopic derivation ==
From the [[kinetic theory of gases]], the pressure exerted by an ideal gas is:
:<math>P = \frac{1}{3}\frac{Nm\langle v^2 \rangle}{V} = \frac{2}{3}\frac{N}{V}\langle K \rangle</math>

With the [[equipartition theorem]], <math>\langle K \rangle = \frac{3}{2}k_B T</math>, yielding <math>PV = Nk_B T</math>.

== Deviations from ideality ==
{{Main|Real gas}}
Real gases deviate from ideal behavior at:
* High pressure (intermolecular forces become significant)
* Low temperature (molecular volume not negligible)

The [[van der Waals equation]] corrects for these effects:
:<math>\left(P + a\frac{n^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = nRT</math>
where <math>a</math> accounts for attraction and <math>b</math> for finite molecular volume.

The [[compressibility factor]] <math>Z = PV/(nRT)</math> measures deviation from ideality: <math>Z = 1</math> for an ideal gas.

== Applications ==
* Calculation of gas density: <math>\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}</math>
* [[Molar mass]] determination
* [[Stoichiometry]] of gaseous reactions
* [[Atmospheric pressure]] modeling

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Kinetic theory of gases]]
* [[Van der Waals equation]]
* [[Boyle's law]]
* [[Gas constant]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Ideal gas law| ]]
[[Category:Gas laws]]
[[Category:Equations of state]]
